params.text:
Removing the fear of complaining

The people you support are often in a vulnerable position, and many worry that if they complain their care will suffer, or that staff will treat them differently. This fear of reprisal is one of the biggest reasons complaints go unspoken. Part of your job is to make it clear, in words and in how you behave, that raising a concern is welcome and will never be held against them.

Complaining should also be genuinely possible for everyone, not just those who are confident and articulate. That means offering information in accessible formats, giving people time, and helping them put their concerns into words if they find that hard.

params.alt: Illustration for Supporting people to complain

params.text:
Advocacy and communication needs

Some people will need extra support to be heard, and you should know where to turn.

  • Independent advocacy: anyone can ask for an independent advocate, and NHS Complaints Advocacy is free and available to help people make a complaint about health or social care.
  • Communication needs: use easy-read leaflets, pictures, interpreters, or communication aids so the person’s own voice comes through.
  • People who know them: with the person’s agreement, family or friends can help express a concern that is hard to put into words.

Never assume someone has no complaint simply because they cannot easily tell you one.

params.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the sentences about supporting people to complain.
params.textField: No one should fear *reprisal* for raising a concern about their care. Where a person struggles to put a complaint into words, an independent *advocate* can speak up on their behalf. Information should be offered in accessible formats such as *easy-read* so everyone can understand it. For someone who does not speak English, an *interpreter* helps them to be heard. Never assume a person who cannot easily speak has no *concern* worth listening to.
